ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Ayam Masak Lemak Cili Api. Mapped to broad class 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific entry for Malaysian coconut-milk chicken curry does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Ayam Masak Lemak Cili Api as a composite dish. Code A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component. The coconut milk gravy and spice matrix are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malaysian composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Ayam Masak Lemak Cili Api is approximately 250 g, comprising a portion of chicken pieces with coconut milk gravy, typically served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Malaysian Foods — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values are sourced from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for traditional Malay coconut-milk chicken dishes. The input record cites HPB Singapore as the primary source. Values for saturated fat are estimated from the known fatty acid profile of coconut milk (approximately 85–90% of total fat as saturated fat) combined with chicken fat composition data from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05064). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data are not reported in the cited source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — analogous estimate for coconut milk chicken curry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Masak Lemak Cili Api. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy to low-GI coconut milk-based curry dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The high fat content from coconut milk (≥15 g/100 g) and protein from chicken significantly delay gastric emptying and glucose absorption, supporting a low GI classification. Carbohydrate content is low (3.2 g/100 g), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 2.8 per 250 g serving (GL = GI/100 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = 35/100 × 8.0 = 2.8). Confidence: 0.70.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (320 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g per meal, request reduced-salt preparation, and consider diluting the coconut milk gravy with water to lower both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and low estimated glycemic index (35), with high fat and protein slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Masak Lemak Cili Api is low in carbohydrates and has a low estimated glycemic index, making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served with a moderate portion of low-GI rice and total caloric intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (estimated ~10.8 g/100 g) predominantly from coconut milk, which may raise LDL cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions (≤150 g), preferring preparations made with reduced-fat coconut milk to limit saturated fat intake to within recommended daily limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric (curcumin) and chili (capsaicin) may provide benefit, but high saturated fat can be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric and bird's eye chili in this dish provide curcumin and capsaicin with documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, the high saturated fat content from coconut milk may counteract these benefits in some individuals, so moderate consumption is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Masak Lemak Cili Api is a quintessential traditional dish originating from Negeri Sembilan, Malaysia, and is one of the most iconic preparations in Malay cuisine. The name translates literally to 'Chicken Cooked in Rich Coconut Milk with Bird's Eye Chilies'. The dish is distinguished by its vibrant golden-yellow colour, derived entirely from fresh turmeric, and its intense fiery heat from a large quantity of cili padi (bird's eye chilies). Uniquely, no cooking oil is used; instead, chicken pieces are simmered directly in a pounded paste of fresh turmeric and chilies combined with rich coconut milk, bruised lemongrass, and asam keping (dried tamarind slices) that provide a characteristic sour note to balance the richness. The resulting gravy is thin, intensely aromatic, and deeply spicy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 185 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 15.2 g fat (predominantly saturated fat from coconut milk, estimated ~10.8 g), and only 3.2 g carbohydrate, giving it a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35. It is halal and gluten-free. Clinically, the low carbohydrate content makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, but the high saturated fat and sodium content (320 mg/100 g) make it a concern for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The turmeric and chili components contribute curcumin and capsaicin, both studied for anti-inflammatory effects. It is traditionally served as a main dish at lunch or dinner alongside steamed white rice, fresh ulam (raw herb salad), or sambal belacan.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as cited in the input record, which is an approved primary source for Malaysian/Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from the known fatty acid profile of coconut milk and chicken fat (USDA SR Legacy), not directly reported. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar coconut milk curry dishes, not a direct measurement. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water content are not available in the cited source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this traditional Negeri Sembilan preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2024. USDA FoodData Central does not contain a direct entry for Ayam Masak Lemak Cili Api. 2. saturated_fat_g (10.8 g/100 g) is estimated from the coconut milk fatty acid profile (approximately 85–90% of total fat as saturated fat per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 12117) combined with chicken fat data (NDB 05064), weighted by approximate recipe proportions. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 4. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the original input is incorrect for a chicken dish and has been removed. 5. Ontology codes are broad-class matches only; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this composite dish. 6. This is a Malaysian dish; Singaporean multilingual requirements (Chinese, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Malay local name is provided.
